attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring or one-time remote agent ("run this every Monday", "open a cleanup PR for X in 2 weeks"), or to manage existing routines. ALSO OFFER PROACTIVELY: after you finish work that has a natural future follow-up, end your reply with a one-line offer to schedule a background agent to do it. Strong signals: a feature flag / gate / experiment / staged rollout was just shipped (offer a one-time agent in ~2 weeks to open a cleanup PR or evaluate results), a new alert/monitor was created (offer a recurring agent to triage it), a TODO/migration with a "remove once X" condition was left behind (offer a one-time agent to do the removal). Skip the offer for refactors, bug fixes, and anything that is done once it ships. Name a concrete action and cadence ("in 2 weeks", "every Monday") and only offer when the run just succeeded — do not pitch a schedule for something that has not happened yet.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- feature-dev:feature-dev: Guided feature development with codebase understanding and architecture focus
- codex:setup: Check whether the local Codex CLI is ready and optionally toggle the stop-time review gate
- codex:rescue: Delegate investigation, an explicit fix request, or follow-up rescue work to the Codex rescue subagent
- lean4:checkpoint: Save progress with a safe commit checkpoint
- lean4:autoprove: Autonomous multi-cycle theorem proving with explicit stop budgets
- lean4:autoformalize: Autonomous end-to-end formalization from informal sources
- lean4:learn: Interactive teaching and mathlib exploration
- lean4:draft: Draft Lean declaration skeletons from informal claims
- lean4:golf: Improve Lean proofs for directness, clarity, performance, and brevity
- lean4:doctor: Diagnostics, cleanup, and migration help
- lean4:formalize: Interactive formalization — drafting plus guided proving
- lean4:review: Read-only code review of Lean proofs
- lean4:refactor: Leverage mathlib, extract helpers, simplify proof strategies
- lean4:prove: Guided cycle-by-cycle theorem proving with explicit checkpoints
- claude-code-setup:claude-automation-recommender: Analyze a codebase and recommend Claude Code automations (hooks, subagents, skills, plugins, MCP servers). Use when user asks for automation recommendations, wants to optimize their Claude Code setup, mentions improving Claude Code workflows, asks how to first set up Claude Code for a project, or wants to know what Claude Code features they should use.
- codex:codex-result-handling: Internal guidance for presenting Codex helper output back to the user
- codex:gpt-5-4-prompting: Internal guidance for composing Codex and GPT-5.4 prompts for coding, review, diagnosis, and research tasks inside the Codex Claude Code plugin
- codex:codex-cli-runtime: Internal helper contract for calling the codex-companion runtime from Claude Code
- lean4:lean4: Use when editing .lean files, debugging Lean 4 builds (type mismatch, sorry, failed to synthesize instance, axiom warnings, lake build errors), searching mathlib for lemmas, formalizing mathematics in Lean, or learning Lean 4 concepts. Also trigger when the user asks for help with Lean 4, mathlib, or lakefile. Do NOT trigger for Coq/Rocq, Agda, Isabelle, HOL4, Mizar, Idris, Megalodon, or other non-Lean theorem provers.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

1	# Outcome refinement for PMF kernels
2	
3	## Status
4	
5	Proposal for a small probability-theory addition to the library: a
6	relation between a one-shot probabilistic specification and a small-step
7	PMF kernel under a finite fuel budget, plus the proof tools needed to
8	establish instances of the relation by induction.
9	
10	The proposal is library-shaped: nothing in it refers to games,
11	sequential interaction, observations, or any specific client. The
12	motivating downstream uses are diverse — relating big-step denotational
13	semantics of probabilistic programs to small-step interpreters, relating
14	abstract Markov chains to concrete state machines, relating compiled
15	representations of probabilistic systems to their source semantics — but
16	the abstraction itself is just about PMFs and iterated kernels.
17	
18	## Definition
19	
20	### Iterated kernel
21	
22	```
23	namespace PMF
24	
25	/-- Iterated PMF kernel: `iter step n b` is the distribution of `step`
26	applied `n` times starting from `b`. -/
27	noncomputable def iter (step : B → PMF B) : Nat → B → PMF B
28	  | 0,     b => PMF.pure b
29	  | n + 1, b => (step b).bind (iter step n)
30	
31	@[simp] theorem iter_zero (step b) : iter step 0 b = PMF.pure b := rfl
32	
33	theorem iter_succ (step b n) :
34	    iter step (n + 1) b = (step b).bind (iter step n) := rfl
35	
36	theorem iter_succ' (step b n) :
37	    iter step (n + 1) b = (iter step n b).bind step := by ...
38	```
39	
40	`iter_succ` and `iter_succ'` correspond to left- and right-iteration;
41	both forms are useful. The combinator is a probabilistic analogue of
42	`Nat.iterate` / `Function.iterate`.
43	
44	### Outcome refinement
45	
46	```
47	/-- An outcome refinement between a one-shot specification semantics
48	and a small-step PMF kernel. -/
49	structure OutcomeRefinement
50	    (A B α : Type) [DecidableEq α] where
51	  /-- One-shot specification distribution. -/
52	  spec    : A → PMF α
53	  /-- Small-step kernel implementing the specification. -/
54	  step    : B → PMF B
55	  /-- Initial state for each spec input. -/
56	  encode  : A → B
57	  /-- Terminal projection. -/
58	  project : B → α
59	  /-- Per-input fuel: how many steps suffice. -/
60	  fuel    : A → Nat
61	  /-- Closure: the implementation realizes the specification. -/
62	  closure : ∀ a,
63	    PMF.map project (PMF.iter step (fuel a) (encode a)) = spec a
64	```
65	
66	The `closure` field is the substantive content. Everything else is
67	data: an encoding, a projection, a fuel function. The role of the
68	structure is to bundle these so downstream lemmas (mass preservation,
69	support results, fuel monotonicity) can be stated once.
70	
71	## Proof tools for closure
72	
73	The following lemmas are the toolkit a client uses to discharge
74	`closure` for a concrete refinement. They are stated as standalone
75	PMF-level facts, not as methods on the structure.
76	
77	### Terminal absorption
78	
79	```
80	/-- If `step b` is the point mass at `b`, iteration stays at `b`. -/
81	theorem iter_of_terminal
82	    {step : B → PMF B} {b : B}
83	    (h : step b = PMF.pure b) (n : Nat) :
84	    PMF.iter step n b = PMF.pure b
85	```
86	
87	The discharge for "encoded final states are terminal".
88	
89	### Successor decomposition
90	
91	```
92	/-- Given a continuation function `next` and a one-step kernel `k`,
93	if the spec satisfies `spec a = (k a).bind (fun x => spec (next a x))`
94	and the implementation satisfies
95	`step (encode a) = (k a).map (encode ∘ next a)`,
96	then closure at `a` reduces to closure at all `next a x`. -/
97	theorem closure_of_succ
98	    {spec : A → PMF α} {step : B → PMF B}
99	    {encode : A → B} {project : B → α} {fuel : A → Nat}
100	    (k : A → PMF X) (next : A → X → A)
101	    (hspec : ∀ a, spec a = (k a).bind (fun x => spec (next a x)))
102	    (himpl : ∀ a, step (encode a) = (k a).map (fun x => encode (next a x)))
103	    (hfuel : ∀ a x, fuel a = fuel (next a x) + 1)
104	    (h_ih : ∀ a x, PMF.map project (PMF.iter step (fuel (next a x))
105	                       (encode (next a x))) = spec (next a x))
106	    (a : A) :
107	    PMF.map project (PMF.iter step (fuel a) (encode a)) = spec a
108	```
109	
110	The standard inductive case. Most clients will discharge `closure` by
111	case-splitting on the structure of `A` and applying `closure_of_succ`
112	once per non-terminal constructor and `iter_of_terminal` for terminal
113	constructors.
114	
115	### Bind compatibility
116	
117	```
118	/-- If the implementation step at `encode a` is a pure-data bind
119	(e.g., a measurable choice with no continuation merging), refinement
120	factors through the bind. -/
121	theorem closure_of_bind ...
122	```
123	
124	A specialization that's frequent enough to deserve its own name —
125	when the spec and implementation share a probabilistic step (for
126	example a public chance node in a transition system), the closure
127	proof at that point is a mechanical bind congruence.
128	
129	## Compositional lemmas
130	
131	```
132	/-- Refinements compose under projection composition. -/
133	theorem OutcomeRefinement.compose
134	    (R : OutcomeRefinement A B α)
135	    (f : α → β) [DecidableEq β] :
136	    OutcomeRefinement A B β
137	```
138	
139	```
140	/-- Fuel monotonicity: extra fuel beyond the closure budget is harmless
141	when iteration becomes absorbing. -/
142	theorem iter_stable_after_terminal
143	    {step : B → PMF B} {b : B} {n m : Nat}
144	    (h : ∀ b' ∈ (PMF.iter step n b).support, step b' = PMF.pure b')
145	    (hle : n ≤ m) :
146	    PMF.iter step m b = PMF.iter step n b
147	```
148	
149	This is the lemma that lets a client run "as long as the fuel allows"
150	and still match a fixed-fuel specification, provided runs become
151	terminal at the spec's fuel.
152	
153	## Suggested location
154	
155	Probably `GameTheory/Math/Probability/Refinement.lean`, alongside the
156	existing `PMFProduct`. A clean filename test: nothing about the file's
157	name or imports should suggest a particular client of the library.
158	
159	If the team prefers a flatter `Math/Probability/` layout that doesn't
160	yet exist, the file would be the natural occasion to introduce it.
161	
162	## What this is not
163	
164	- Not a game-theoretic concept. There is no notion of player, action,
165	  or observation in the abstraction. The abstraction is about
166	  probabilistic kernels and iterated rewriting; nothing more.
167	- Not a probabilistic bisimulation. Standard probabilistic
168	  bisimulation symmetrically relates two systems' step distributions.
169	  Outcome refinement is asymmetric and only constrains the terminal
170	  projection. Both views are useful; they are different relations.
171	- Not specific to PMF. The same shape works for any commutative monad
172	  with a notion of equality of distributions; we restrict to `PMF`
173	  because that is what the existing library uses and decidable
174	  equality on the carrier is enough. A monad-generic version is a
175	  later refactor opportunity, not a prerequisite.
176	
177	## Suggested API surface
178	
179	Minimum viable additions:
180	
181	```
182	namespace PMF
183	def iter
184	theorem iter_zero
185	theorem iter_succ
186	theorem iter_succ'
187	theorem iter_of_terminal
188	theorem iter_stable_after_terminal
189	end PMF
190	
191	namespace Math.Probability  -- or whatever the host namespace is
192	structure OutcomeRefinement
193	theorem OutcomeRefinement.compose
194	theorem closure_of_succ
195	theorem closure_of_bind
196	end Math.Probability
197	```
198	
199	That is enough to express and discharge the abstraction. Everything
200	beyond is convenience.
201	
202	## Why this belongs in the library, not in a client
203	
204	A standalone client could define `iter` and `OutcomeRefinement`
205	locally. The reason to lift them is the following: the closure proof
206	of any concrete instance is the kind of probability calculation that
207	belongs in a probability library — bind associativity, map–bind
208	exchange, support inclusions, mass invariance. Once `iter` exists as
209	a named primitive, the lemmas below it become broadly useful (e.g.,
210	"iter respects support", "iter respects mass under normalized step").
211	Hiding the primitive inside one client costs the rest of the
212	ecosystem those lemmas.
213	
214	The corresponding code currently sits inside one client's proof tree
215	as inlined intermediate facts. Naming the primitive lets the client
216	stop inlining; the proof tree shrinks; future clients reuse the
217	lemmas.
218	
219	## Out of scope for this proposal
220	
221	- A monad-generic version (later).
222	- Refinement up to a relation rather than equality of projections.
223	  Useful for refinement of randomized algorithms with stuttering;
224	  not needed for the immediate use cases.
225	- Coinductive / infinite-fuel variants. The current proposal is
226	  fuel-bounded; it does not say anything about non-terminating
227	  small-step systems. Adding that would require ergodic-theoretic
228	  hypotheses outside the scope of this addition.
229

file.content:
# Outcome refinement for PMF kernels

## Status

Proposal for a small probability-theory addition to the library: a
relation between a one-shot probabilistic specification and a small-step
PMF kernel under a finite fuel budget, plus the proof tools needed to
establish instances of the relation by induction.

The proposal is library-shaped: nothing in it refers to games,
sequential interaction, observations, or any specific client. The
motivating downstream uses are diverse — relating big-step denotational
semantics of probabilistic programs to small-step interpreters, relating
abstract Markov chains to concrete state machines, relating compiled
representations of probabilistic systems to their source semantics — but
the abstraction itself is just about PMFs and iterated kernels.

## Definition

### Iterated kernel

```
namespace PMF

/-- Iterated PMF kernel: `iter step n b` is the distribution of `step`
applied `n` times starting from `b`. -/
noncomputable def iter (step : B → PMF B) : Nat → B → PMF B
  | 0,     b => PMF.pure b
  | n + 1, b => (step b).bind (iter step n)

@[simp] theorem iter_zero (step b) : iter step 0 b = PMF.pure b := rfl

theorem iter_succ (step b n) :
    iter step (n + 1) b = (step b).bind (iter step n) := rfl

theorem iter_succ' (step b n) :
    iter step (n + 1) b = (iter step n b).bind step := by ...
```

`iter_succ` and `iter_succ'` correspond to left- and right-iteration;
both forms are useful. The combinator is a probabilistic analogue of
`Nat.iterate` / `Function.iterate`.

### Outcome refinement

```
/-- An outcome refinement between a one-shot specification semantics
and a small-step PMF kernel. -/
structure OutcomeRefinement
    (A B α : Type) [DecidableEq α] where
  /-- One-shot specification distribution. -/
  spec    : A → PMF α
  /-- Small-step kernel implementing the specification. -/
  step    : B → PMF B
  /-- Initial state for each spec input. -/
  encode  : A → B
  /-- Terminal projection. -/
  project : B → α
  /-- Per-input fuel: how many steps suffice. -/
  fuel    : A → Nat
  /-- Closure: the implementation realizes the specification. -/
  closure : ∀ a,
    PMF.map project (PMF.iter step (fuel a) (encode a)) = spec a
```

The `closure` field is the substantive content. Everything else is
data: an encoding, a projection, a fuel function. The role of the
structure is to bundle these so downstream lemmas (mass preservation,
support results, fuel monotonicity) can be stated once.

## Proof tools for closure

The following lemmas are the toolkit a client uses to discharge
`closure` for a concrete refinement. They are stated as standalone
PMF-level facts, not as methods on the structure.

### Terminal absorption

```
/-- If `step b` is the point mass at `b`, iteration stays at `b`. -/
theorem iter_of_terminal
    {step : B → PMF B} {b : B}
    (h : step b = PMF.pure b) (n : Nat) :
    PMF.iter step n b = PMF.pure b
```

The discharge for "encoded final states are terminal".

### Successor decomposition

```
/-- Given a continuation function `next` and a one-step kernel `k`,
if the spec satisfies `spec a = (k a).bind (fun x => spec (next a x))`
and the implementation satisfies
`step (encode a) = (k a).map (encode ∘ next a)`,
then closure at `a` reduces to closure at all `next a x`. -/
theorem closure_of_succ
    {spec : A → PMF α} {step : B → PMF B}
    {encode : A → B} {project : B → α} {fuel : A → Nat}
    (k : A → PMF X) (next : A → X → A)
    (hspec : ∀ a, spec a = (k a).bind (fun x => spec (next a x)))
    (himpl : ∀ a, step (encode a) = (k a).map (fun x => encode (next a x)))
    (hfuel : ∀ a x, fuel a = fuel (next a x) + 1)
    (h_ih : ∀ a x, PMF.map project (PMF.iter step (fuel (next a x))
                       (encode (next a x))) = spec (next a x))
    (a : A) :
    PMF.map project (PMF.iter step (fuel a) (encode a)) = spec a
```

The standard inductive case. Most clients will discharge `closure` by
case-splitting on the structure of `A` and applying `closure_of_succ`
once per non-terminal constructor and `iter_of_terminal` for terminal
constructors.

### Bind compatibility

```
/-- If the implementation step at `encode a` is a pure-data bind
(e.g., a measurable choice with no continuation merging), refinement
factors through the bind. -/
theorem closure_of_bind ...
```

A specialization that's frequent enough to deserve its own name —
when the spec and implementation share a probabilistic step (for
example a public chance node in a transition system), the closure
proof at that point is a mechanical bind congruence.

## Compositional lemmas

```
/-- Refinements compose under projection composition. -/
theorem OutcomeRefinement.compose
    (R : OutcomeRefinement A B α)
    (f : α → β) [DecidableEq β] :
    OutcomeRefinement A B β
```

```
/-- Fuel monotonicity: extra fuel beyond the closure budget is harmless
when iteration becomes absorbing. -/
theorem iter_stable_after_terminal
    {step : B → PMF B} {b : B} {n m : Nat}
    (h : ∀ b' ∈ (PMF.iter step n b).support, step b' = PMF.pure b')
    (hle : n ≤ m) :
    PMF.iter step m b = PMF.iter step n b
```

This is the lemma that lets a client run "as long as the fuel allows"
and still match a fixed-fuel specification, provided runs become
terminal at the spec's fuel.

## Suggested location

Probably `GameTheory/Math/Probability/Refinement.lean`, alongside the
existing `PMFProduct`. A clean filename test: nothing about the file's
name or imports should suggest a particular client of the library.

If the team prefers a flatter `Math/Probability/` layout that doesn't
yet exist, the file would be the natural occasion to introduce it.

## What this is not

- Not a game-theoretic concept. There is no notion of player, action,
  or observation in the abstraction. The abstraction is about
  probabilistic kernels and iterated rewriting; nothing more.
- Not a probabilistic bisimulation. Standard probabilistic
  bisimulation symmetrically relates two systems' step distributions.
  Outcome refinement is asymmetric and only constrains the terminal
  projection. Both views are useful; they are different relations.
- Not specific to PMF. The same shape works for any commutative monad
  with a notion of equality of distributions; we restrict to `PMF`
  because that is what the existing library uses and decidable
  equality on the carrier is enough. A monad-generic version is a
  later refactor opportunity, not a prerequisite.

## Suggested API surface

Minimum viable additions:

```
namespace PMF
def iter
theorem iter_zero
theorem iter_succ
theorem iter_succ'
theorem iter_of_terminal
theorem iter_stable_after_terminal
end PMF

namespace Math.Probability  -- or whatever the host namespace is
structure OutcomeRefinement
theorem OutcomeRefinement.compose
theorem closure_of_succ
theorem closure_of_bind
end Math.Probability
```

That is enough to express and discharge the abstraction. Everything
beyond is convenience.

## Why this belongs in the library, not in a client

A standalone client could define `iter` and `OutcomeRefinement`
locally. The reason to lift them is the following: the closure proof
of any concrete instance is the kind of probability calculation that
belongs in a probability library — bind associativity, map–bind
exchange, support inclusions, mass invariance. Once `iter` exists as
a named primitive, the lemmas below it become broadly useful (e.g.,
"iter respects support", "iter respects mass under normalized step").
Hiding the primitive inside one client costs the rest of the
ecosystem those lemmas.

The corresponding code currently sits inside one client's proof tree
as inlined intermediate facts. Naming the primitive lets the client
stop inlining; the proof tree shrinks; future clients reuse the
lemmas.

## Out of scope for this proposal

- A monad-generic version (later).
- Refinement up to a relation rather than equality of projections.
  Useful for refinement of randomized algorithms with stuttering;
  not needed for the immediate use cases.
- Coinductive / infinite-fuel variants. The current proposal is
  fuel-bounded; it does not say anything about non-terminating
  small-step systems. Adding that would require ergodic-theoretic
  hypotheses outside the scope of this addition.

content.text:
I've read OUTCOME_REFINEMENT.md. It's a 229-line proposal for adding a probability-theory abstraction to the library: a `PMF.iter` iterated kernel combinator and an `OutcomeRefinement` structure relating one-shot specs to small-step PMF kernels under finite fuel, plus a toolkit (`iter_of_terminal`, `closure_of_succ`, `closure_of_bind`, `iter_stable_after_terminal`, composition) for discharging closure proofs by induction.

Key points:
- **Library-shaped**: no game-theory references; just PMF + iterated kernels.
- **Suggested location**: `GameTheory/Math/Probability/Refinement.lean`, alongside `PMFProduct`.
- **Motivation**: closure proofs currently inlined in one client's proof tree should be lifted so the primitive (`iter`) and its lemmas become reusable.
- **Out of scope**: monad-generic version, refinement up to a relation, coinductive/infinite-fuel variants.

1	import Math.ProbabilityMassFunction
2	import Math.PMFProduct
3	
4	/-! # Tower property for parameterized Markov chains
5	
6	Given a distribution `ν` over parameters `P` and a parameterized step function
7	`step : P → List S → PMF S`, we show that the averaged ("mixed") run can be
8	realized by a single non-parameterized chain whose transitions condition `ν`
9	on the observed trace at each step.
10	
11	This is the abstract core of Kuhn's theorem: conditioning distributes over
12	sequential Kleisli composition.
13	-/
14	
15	set_option autoImplicit false
16	
17	namespace Math.ParameterizedChain
18	
19	open Math.ProbabilityMassFunction
20	
21	variable {P S : Type*}
22	
23	/-- Run a parameterized Markov chain for `k` steps from initial state `s₀`,
24	recording the full state trace. -/
25	noncomputable def pureRun (step : P → List S → PMF S) (s₀ : S)
26	    (k : Nat) (π : P) : PMF (List S) :=
27	  Nat.rec (PMF.pure [s₀])
28	    (fun _ rec =>
29	      rec.bind (fun ss =>
30	        pushforward (step π ss) (fun t => ss ++ [t])))
31	    k
32	
33	theorem append_singleton_inj {α : Type*} {as bs : List α} {a b : α}
34	    (h : as ++ [a] = bs ++ [b]) : as = bs ∧ a = b :=
35	  ⟨List.append_inj_left' h (by simp),
36	   by have := List.append_inj_right' h (by simp); simpa using this⟩
37	
38	open Classical in
39	/-- At successor step, `pureRun` decomposes as prefix reach times one-step transition. -/
40	theorem pureRun_succ_append (step : P → List S → PMF S) (s₀ : S)
41	    (k : Nat) (π : P) (ss : List S) (t : S) :
42	    pureRun step s₀ (k + 1) π (ss ++ [t]) =
43	      pureRun step s₀ k π ss * step π ss t := by
44	  change (pureRun step s₀ k π).bind
45	    (fun ss' => pushforward (step π ss') (fun t' => ss' ++ [t'])) (ss ++ [t]) = _
46	  rw [PMF.bind_apply]
47	  have hterm : ∀ ss' : List S, ss' ≠ ss →
48	      pureRun step s₀ k π ss' *
49	        (pushforward (step π ss') (fun t' => ss' ++ [t'])) (ss ++ [t]) = 0 := by
50	    intro ss' hne
51	    suffices (pushforward (step π ss') (fun t' => ss' ++ [t'])) (ss ++ [t]) = 0 by
52	      rw [this, mul_zero]
53	    simp only [pushforward, PMF.bind_apply, PMF.pure_apply]
54	    rw [ENNReal.tsum_eq_zero]
55	    intro t'
56	    rw [if_neg (fun h => hne (append_singleton_inj h).1.symm), mul_zero]
57	  rw [tsum_eq_single ss (fun ss' hne => hterm ss' hne)]
58	  congr 1
59	  simp only [pushforward, PMF.bind_apply, PMF.pure_apply]
60	  rw [tsum_eq_single t (fun t' ht' => by
61	    rw [if_neg (fun h => ht' (append_singleton_inj h).2.symm), mul_zero])]
62	  simp
63	
64	open Classical in
65	/-- `pureRun` at successor step assigns zero probability to the empty trace. -/
66	theorem pureRun_succ_nil (step : P → List S → PMF S) (s₀ : S) (k : Nat) (π : P) :
67	    pureRun step s₀ (k + 1) π [] = 0 := by
68	  change (pureRun step s₀ k π).bind
69	    (fun ss => pushforward (step π ss) (fun t => ss ++ [t])) [] = 0
70	  simp only [PMF.bind_apply]
71	  rw [ENNReal.tsum_eq_zero]
72	  intro ss
73	  suffices (pushforward (step π ss) (fun t => ss ++ [t])) [] = 0 by rw [this, mul_zero]
74	  simp only [pushforward, PMF.bind_apply, PMF.pure_apply]
75	  rw [ENNReal.tsum_eq_zero]
76	  intro t
77	  simp [show ([] : List S) ≠ ss ++ [t] from List.ne_nil_of_length_pos (by simp)]
78	
79	open Classical in
80	/-- Traces with nonzero `pureRun` probability have length exactly `n + 1`. -/

import Math.ProbabilityMassFunction
import Math.PMFProduct

/-! # Tower property for parameterized Markov chains

Given a distribution `ν` over parameters `P` and a parameterized step function
`step : P → List S → PMF S`, we show that the averaged ("mixed") run can be
realized by a single non-parameterized chain whose transitions condition `ν`
on the observed trace at each step.

This is the abstract core of Kuhn's theorem: conditioning distributes over
sequential Kleisli composition.
-/

set_option autoImplicit false

namespace Math.ParameterizedChain

open Math.ProbabilityMassFunction

variable {P S : Type*}

/-- Run a parameterized Markov chain for `k` steps from initial state `s₀`,
recording the full state trace. -/
noncomputable def pureRun (step : P → List S → PMF S) (s₀ : S)
    (k : Nat) (π : P) : PMF (List S) :=
  Nat.rec (PMF.pure [s₀])
    (fun _ rec =>
      rec.bind (fun ss =>
        pushforward (step π ss) (fun t => ss ++ [t])))
    k

theorem append_singleton_inj {α : Type*} {as bs : List α} {a b : α}
    (h : as ++ [a] = bs ++ [b]) : as = bs ∧ a = b :=
  ⟨List.append_inj_left' h (by simp),
   by have := List.append_inj_right' h (by simp); simpa using this⟩

open Classical in
/-- At successor step, `pureRun` decomposes as prefix reach times one-step transition. -/
theorem pureRun_succ_append (step : P → List S → PMF S) (s₀ : S)
    (k : Nat) (π : P) (ss : List S) (t : S) :
    pureRun step s₀ (k + 1) π (ss ++ [t]) =
      pureRun step s₀ k π ss * step π ss t := by
  change (pureRun step s₀ k π).bind
    (fun ss' => pushforward (step π ss') (fun t' => ss' ++ [t'])) (ss ++ [t]) = _
  rw [PMF.bind_apply]
  have hterm : ∀ ss' : List S, ss' ≠ ss →
      pureRun step s₀ k π ss' *
        (pushforward (step π ss') (fun t' => ss' ++ [t'])) (ss ++ [t]) = 0 := by
    intro ss' hne
    suffices (pushforward (step π ss') (fun t' => ss' ++ [t'])) (ss ++ [t]) = 0 by
      rw [this, mul_zero]
    simp only [pushforward, PMF.bind_apply, PMF.pure_apply]
    rw [ENNReal.tsum_eq_zero]
    intro t'
    rw [if_neg (fun h => hne (append_singleton_inj h).1.symm), mul_zero]
  rw [tsum_eq_single ss (fun ss' hne => hterm ss' hne)]
  congr 1
  simp only [pushforward, PMF.bind_apply, PMF.pure_apply]
  rw [tsum_eq_single t (fun t' ht' => by
    rw [if_neg (fun h => ht' (append_singleton_inj h).2.symm), mul_zero])]
  simp

open Classical in
/-- `pureRun` at successor step assigns zero probability to the empty trace. -/
theorem pureRun_succ_nil (step : P → List S → PMF S) (s₀ : S) (k : Nat) (π : P) :
    pureRun step s₀ (k + 1) π [] = 0 := by
  change (pureRun step s₀ k π).bind
    (fun ss => pushforward (step π ss) (fun t => ss ++ [t])) [] = 0
  simp only [PMF.bind_apply]
  rw [ENNReal.tsum_eq_zero]
  intro ss
  suffices (pushforward (step π ss) (fun t => ss ++ [t])) [] = 0 by rw [this, mul_zero]
  simp only [pushforward, PMF.bind_apply, PMF.pure_apply]
  rw [ENNReal.tsum_eq_zero]
  intro t
  simp [show ([] : List S) ≠ ss ++ [t] from List.ne_nil_of_length_pos (by simp)]

open Classical in
/-- Traces with nonzero `pureRun` probability have length exactly `n + 1`. -/

1	import GameTheory.Languages.EFG.Syntax
2	import Mathlib.Tactic.Linarith
3	
4	/-!
5	# Concrete Sequential Refinements for Extensive-Form Games
6	
7	Concrete definitions for subgame perfection and related refinements,
8	building on the schema-based definitions in `EFG.lean`.
9	
10	## Main definitions
11	
12	- `IsSubgame` — concrete subgame identification (informationally closed subtree)
13	- `IsSubgamePerfectEq` — subgame-perfect equilibrium using the concrete predicate
14	- `IsPerfectInfo` — perfect-information trees (each info-set appears at most once)
15	
16	## Main theorems
17	
18	- `isSubgame_root` — the root is always a subgame of itself
19	- `isSubgame_terminal` — terminal nodes are subgames (when reachable)
20	- `spe_implies_nash` — SPE implies Nash equilibrium in the strategic form
21	- `spe_implies_isNash` — SPE implies Nash (EU formulation)
22	- `perfectInfo_isSubgame_decision` — in perfect-info games, every decision node
23	  starts a subgame
24	- Entry deterrence game: SPE and non-SPE Nash verification
25	-/
26	
27	namespace EFG
28	
29	open GameTheory
30	open Math.Probability
31	
32	-- ============================================================================
33	-- Helper lemmas
34	-- ============================================================================
35	
36	theorem decisionNodeIn_reachBy {S : InfoStructure} {Outcome : Type}
37	    {p : S.Player} {I : S.Infoset p} {t : GameTree S Outcome}
38	    (h : DecisionNodeIn I t) :
39	    ∃ (hist : List (HistoryStep S)) (next : S.Act I → GameTree S Outcome),
40	      ReachBy hist t (.decision I next) := by
41	  induction h with
42	  | root next => exact ⟨[], next, .here _⟩
43	  | in_chance k μ hk next' b _hIn ih =>
44	    obtain ⟨hist, next, hReach⟩ := ih
45	    exact ⟨HistoryStep.chance k b :: hist, next, .chance b hReach⟩
46	  | in_decision I' next' a _hIn ih =>
47	    obtain ⟨hist, next, hReach⟩ := ih
48	    exact ⟨HistoryStep.action _ I' a :: hist, next, .action a hReach⟩
49	
50	theorem reachBy_deterministic {S : InfoStructure} {Outcome : Type}
51	    {h : List (HistoryStep S)} {root t₁ t₂ : GameTree S Outcome}
52	    (hr₁ : ReachBy h root t₁) (hr₂ : ReachBy h root t₂) : t₁ = t₂ := by
53	  induction hr₁ generalizing t₂ with
54	  | nil =>
55	      cases hr₂
56	      rfl
57	  | @cons a rest s u t₁ hstep₁ htail₁ ih =>
58	      cases hr₂ with
59	      | @cons _ _ _ u₂ _ hstep₂ htail₂ =>
60	          have hu : u = u₂ := by
61	            cases a with
62	            | chance k b =>
63	                rcases hstep₁ with ⟨μ₁, hk₁, next₁, hs₁, hu₁⟩
64	                rcases hstep₂ with ⟨μ₂, hk₂, next₂, hs₂, hu₂⟩
65	                cases hs₁
66	                cases hs₂
67	                simp [hu₁, hu₂]
68	            | action p I a =>
69	                rcases hstep₁ with ⟨next₁, hs₁, hu₁⟩
70	                rcases hstep₂ with ⟨next₂, hs₂, hu₂⟩
71	                cases hs₁
72	                cases hs₂
73	                simp [hu₁, hu₂]
74	          subst hu
75	          exact ih htail₂
76	
77	-- ============================================================================
78	-- Concrete subgame identification
79	-- ============================================================================
80

file.content:
import GameTheory.Languages.EFG.Syntax
import Mathlib.Tactic.Linarith

/-!
# Concrete Sequential Refinements for Extensive-Form Games

Concrete definitions for subgame perfection and related refinements,
building on the schema-based definitions in `EFG.lean`.

## Main definitions

- `IsSubgame` — concrete subgame identification (informationally closed subtree)
- `IsSubgamePerfectEq` — subgame-perfect equilibrium using the concrete predicate
- `IsPerfectInfo` — perfect-information trees (each info-set appears at most once)

## Main theorems

- `isSubgame_root` — the root is always a subgame of itself
- `isSubgame_terminal` — terminal nodes are subgames (when reachable)
- `spe_implies_nash` — SPE implies Nash equilibrium in the strategic form
- `spe_implies_isNash` — SPE implies Nash (EU formulation)
- `perfectInfo_isSubgame_decision` — in perfect-info games, every decision node
  starts a subgame
- Entry deterrence game: SPE and non-SPE Nash verification
-/

namespace EFG

open GameTheory
open Math.Probability

-- ============================================================================
-- Helper lemmas
-- ============================================================================

theorem decisionNodeIn_reachBy {S : InfoStructure} {Outcome : Type}
    {p : S.Player} {I : S.Infoset p} {t : GameTree S Outcome}
    (h : DecisionNodeIn I t) :
    ∃ (hist : List (HistoryStep S)) (next : S.Act I → GameTree S Outcome),
      ReachBy hist t (.decision I next) := by
  induction h with
  | root next => exact ⟨[], next, .here _⟩
  | in_chance k μ hk next' b _hIn ih =>
    obtain ⟨hist, next, hReach⟩ := ih
    exact ⟨HistoryStep.chance k b :: hist, next, .chance b hReach⟩
  | in_decision I' next' a _hIn ih =>
    obtain ⟨hist, next, hReach⟩ := ih
    exact ⟨HistoryStep.action _ I' a :: hist, next, .action a hReach⟩

theorem reachBy_deterministic {S : InfoStructure} {Outcome : Type}
    {h : List (HistoryStep S)} {root t₁ t₂ : GameTree S Outcome}
    (hr₁ : ReachBy h root t₁) (hr₂ : ReachBy h root t₂) : t₁ = t₂ := by
  induction hr₁ generalizing t₂ with
  | nil =>
      cases hr₂
      rfl
  | @cons a rest s u t₁ hstep₁ htail₁ ih =>
      cases hr₂ with
      | @cons _ _ _ u₂ _ hstep₂ htail₂ =>
          have hu : u = u₂ := by
            cases a with
            | chance k b =>
                rcases hstep₁ with ⟨μ₁, hk₁, next₁, hs₁, hu₁⟩
                rcases hstep₂ with ⟨μ₂, hk₂, next₂, hs₂, hu₂⟩
                cases hs₁
                cases hs₂
                simp [hu₁, hu₂]
            | action p I a =>
                rcases hstep₁ with ⟨next₁, hs₁, hu₁⟩
                rcases hstep₂ with ⟨next₂, hs₂, hu₂⟩
                cases hs₁
                cases hs₂
                simp [hu₁, hu₂]
          subst hu
          exact ih htail₂

-- ============================================================================
-- Concrete subgame identification
-- ============================================================================

end PreKernelStep

/-- A finite-horizon sequential game built from a repeated semantic step. -/
structure StepwiseGame (ι : Type) [Fintype ι] where
  Step : PreKernelStep ι
  init : PMF Step.State
  horizon : Nat
  utility : Step.State → Payoff ι

namespace StepwiseGame

variable (G : StepwiseGame ι)

/-- Run distribution after `horizon` repeated steps under policy `π`. -/
noncomputable def runDist (π : Policy G.Step) : PMF G.Step.State :=
  Nat.rec G.init (fun _ acc => acc.bind (G.Step.stepKernel π)) G.horizon

@[simp] theorem runDist_zero (π : Policy G.Step) (h0 : G.horizon = 0) :
    G.runDist π = G.init := by
  simp [runDist, h0]

/-- Collapse a sequential game into its profile-to-outcome kernel semantics. -/
noncomputable def toKernelGame : KernelGame ι where
  Strategy := fun i => G.Step.View i → PMF (G.Step.Act i)
  Outcome := G.Step.State
  utility := G.utility
  outcomeKernel := fun π => G.runDist π

@[simp] theorem toKernelGame_outcomeKernel (π : Policy G.Step) :
    G.toKernelGame.outcomeKernel π = G.runDist π := rfl

end StepwiseGame

section DeterministicPolicy

variable {A : ι → Type} [∀ i, Fintype (A i)]

open Classical in
/-- Product of point masses is a point mass at the joint assignment. -/
theorem pmfPi_pure (σ : ∀ i, A i) :
    pmfPi (fun i => (PMF.pure (σ i) : PMF (A i))) = PMF.pure σ := by
  classical
  ext s
  by_cases hs : s = σ
  · subst hs
    simp [pmfPi_apply]
  · have hneq : ¬ ∀ i, s i = σ i := by
      intro hall
      apply hs
      funext i
      exact hall i
    obtain ⟨i, hi⟩ := not_forall.mp hneq
    have hfactor0 : (if s i = σ i then (1 : ENNReal) else 0) = 0 := by
      simp [hi]
    have hprod0 :
        (∏ x : ι, (if s x = σ x then (1 : ENNReal) else 0)) = 0 := by
      calc
        (∏ x : ι, (if s x = σ x then (1 : ENNReal) else 0))
            = (if s i = σ i then (1 : ENNReal) else 0) *
              (Finset.prod (Finset.univ.erase i)
                (fun x => (if s x = σ x then (1 : ENNReal) else 0))) := by
                simpa [Finset.mem_univ] using
                  (Finset.mul_prod_erase
                    (s := (Finset.univ : Finset ι))
                    (f := fun x => (if s x = σ x then (1 : ENNReal) else 0))
                    (a := i) (by simp)).symm
        _ = 0 := by simp [hfactor0]
    simpa [pmfPi_apply, PMF.pure_apply, hs] using hprod0

variable (K : PreKernelStep ι)

/-- Deterministic (pure) policy from a joint action assignment. -/
noncomputable def constPurePolicy (σ : ∀ i, K.Act i) : Policy K :=
  fun i _ => PMF.pure (σ i)

@[simp] theorem stepKernel_constPure (σ : ∀ i, K.Act i) (s : K.State) :
    K.stepKernel (constPurePolicy K σ) s = K.combine s σ := by
  simp [PreKernelStep.stepKernel, PreKernelStep.jointActDist, constPurePolicy, pmfPi_pure]

end DeterministicPolicy

section KernelEmbedding

/-- A `KernelGame` as a 1-step sequential game with trivial views. -/
noncomputable def KernelGame.toOneStepStepwise (ι : Type) [Fintype ι]
    (Gk : KernelGame ι) [∀ i, Fintype (Gk.Strategy i)] : StepwiseGame ι where
  Step := {
    State := Option Gk.Outcome
    View := fun _ => PUnit
    Act := Gk.Strategy
    observe := fun _ _ => PUnit.unit
    combine := fun s σ =>
      match s with
      | none => (Gk.outcomeKernel σ).bind (fun ω => PMF.pure (some ω))
      | some ω => PMF.pure (some ω)
  }
  init := PMF.pure none
  horizon := 1

content.text:
Mixed: the `iter` half is clearly generic and earns its keep — there are already 5+ inlined `Nat.rec`/`Nat.iterate` PMF chains across `ParameterizedChain.lean`, `MAID/SOS.lean`, `MAID/FrontierEval.lean`, `InfoModel/SemanticForm.lean`, `MultiRound/Compile.lean`, and `Kuhn/KuhnModel.lean` that would all consume `PMF.iter` and the iter_* lemmas. The `OutcomeRefinement` structure plus `closure_of_succ`/`closure_of_bind` is shakier — the doc admits it currently has exactly one client, and the per-input `fuel : A → Nat` shape is opinionated (most existing chains here use a global horizon, not a per-input fuel), so the abstraction may not survive contact with a second consumer.

Suggested split: lift `iter`, `iter_zero/succ/succ'`, `iter_of_terminal`, `iter_stable_after_terminal` now into `Math/Probability/Iter.lean`; defer `OutcomeRefinement` until a second client materializes that confirms the fuel/encode/project shape.

file.content:
import Mathlib.Data.Real.Basic
import Mathlib.Data.NNReal.Basic
import Mathlib.Probability.ProbabilityMassFunction.Monad
import Mathlib.Probability.ProbabilityMassFunction.Constructions

/-!
# Math.Probability

Stochastic kernels and expected-value infrastructure for discrete game theory.

Provides:
- `Kernel α β` — stochastic kernels (Markov kernels) using Mathlib's `PMF`
- `Kernel.id`, `Kernel.comp`, `Kernel.pushforward`, `Kernel.linExt`,
  `Kernel.ofFun` — basic operations
- `expect` — expected value of a real-valued function under a `PMF`
- Utility lemmas: `expect_pure`, `expect_bind`, `expect_const`, `expect_eq_sum`

-/

namespace Math
namespace Probability

-- ============================================================================
-- Kernels (using Mathlib's PMF)
-- ============================================================================

/-- A stochastic kernel from `α` to `β`: maps each input to a PMF over outputs. -/
abbrev Kernel (α β : Type) : Type := α → PMF β

namespace Kernel

/-- Identity kernel. -/
noncomputable def id (α : Type) : Kernel α α := PMF.pure

/-- Kernel composition (Kleisli composition). -/
noncomputable def comp (k₁ : Kernel α β) (k₂ : Kernel β γ) : Kernel α γ :=
  fun a => (k₁ a).bind k₂

/-- Pushforward of a kernel to input distributions. -/
noncomputable def pushforward (k : Kernel α β) : PMF α → PMF β :=
  fun μ => μ.bind k

/-- Linear-extension notation for `pushforward`. -/
noncomputable def linExt (k : Kernel α β) : PMF α → PMF β := Kernel.pushforward k

/-- Pushforward along a pure function (deterministic kernel). -/
noncomputable def ofFun (f : α → β) : Kernel α β := fun a => PMF.pure (f a)


@[simp] theorem comp_apply (k₁ : Kernel α β) (k₂ : Kernel β γ) (a : α) :

12	marginals, conditioning, disintegration, coordinate independence.

27	-- ============================================================================

29	private lemma pmf_sum_eq_one {α : Type*} [Fintype α] (μ : PMF α) :
30	    ∑ a : α, μ a = 1 := by
31	  have h := PMF.tsum_coe μ
32	  rwa [tsum_eq_sum (s := Finset.univ)
33	    (fun x hx => absurd (Finset.mem_univ x) hx)] at h

38	variable {A : ι → Type uA}

41	lemma prod_factor_erase [Fintype ι] (σ : ∀ i, PMF (A i)) (j : ι) (s : ∀ i, A i) :
42	    (∏ i : ι, σ i (s i))
43	      = (σ j) (s j) * (∏ i ∈ (Finset.univ.erase j), σ i (s i)) := by
44	  classical
45	  simpa [Finset.mem_univ] using
46	    (Finset.mul_prod_erase (s := (Finset.univ : Finset ι))
47	      (f := fun i => σ i (s i)) (a := j) (by simp)).symm
48	
49	/-- Updating at coordinate `j` does not change the product over `univ.erase j`. -/
50	lemma prod_erase_update_eq [Fintype ι]
51	    (σ : ∀ i, PMF (A i)) (j : ι) (s : ∀ i, A i) (a : A j) :
52	    (∏ i ∈ Finset.univ.erase j, σ i ((Function.update s j a) i))

54	  apply Finset.prod_congr rfl

63	  intro ⟨a, s⟩
64	  apply Prod.ext

68	    · simp [swapJA, Function.update]

import Mathlib.Data.Fintype.Basic
import Mathlib.Algebra.BigOperators.Ring.Finset
import Mathlib.Algebra.BigOperators.Group.Finset.Basic
import Mathlib.Probability.ProbabilityMassFunction.Constructions
import Math.FunctionUpdate
import Math.ProbabilityMassFunction

/-!
# Independent Product Distributions

Independent product distributions (`pmfPi`) and their coordinate-level operations:
marginals, conditioning, disintegration, coordinate independence.
Re-exports pushforward from `ProbabilityMassFunction.lean`.
-/

namespace Math
namespace PMFProduct

open scoped BigOperators

universe uι uA uα uβ uγ

set_option autoImplicit false

-- ============================================================================
-- Auxiliary Helpers
-- ============================================================================

private lemma pmf_sum_eq_one {α : Type*} [Fintype α] (μ : PMF α) :
    ∑ a : α, μ a = 1 := by
  have h := PMF.tsum_coe μ
  rwa [tsum_eq_sum (s := Finset.univ)
    (fun x hx => absurd (Finset.mem_univ x) hx)] at h

section Aux

variable {ι : Type uι} [DecidableEq ι]
variable {A : ι → Type uA}

/-- Factor the product weight into the `j`-coordinate and the rest. -/
lemma prod_factor_erase [Fintype ι] (σ : ∀ i, PMF (A i)) (j : ι) (s : ∀ i, A i) :
    (∏ i : ι, σ i (s i))
      = (σ j) (s j) * (∏ i ∈ (Finset.univ.erase j), σ i (s i)) := by
  classical
  simpa [Finset.mem_univ] using
    (Finset.mul_prod_erase (s := (Finset.univ : Finset ι))
      (f := fun i => σ i (s i)) (a := j) (by simp)).symm

/-- Updating at coordinate `j` does not change the product over `univ.erase j`. -/
lemma prod_erase_update_eq [Fintype ι]
    (σ : ∀ i, PMF (A i)) (j : ι) (s : ∀ i, A i) (a : A j) :
    (∏ i ∈ Finset.univ.erase j, σ i ((Function.update s j a) i))
      = (∏ i ∈ Finset.univ.erase j, σ i (s i)) := by
  apply Finset.prod_congr rfl
  intro i hi
  simp [Function.update, Finset.ne_of_mem_erase hi]

/-- Swap outer `a` with the `j`-coordinate of `s`, and update `s` at `j` to `a`. -/
def swapJA (j : ι) : (A j × (∀ i, A i)) → (A j × (∀ i, A i)) :=
  fun p => (p.2 j, Function.update p.2 j p.1)

lemma swapJA_involutive (j : ι) : Function.Involutive (swapJA (A := A) j) := by
  intro ⟨a, s⟩
  apply Prod.ext
  · simp [swapJA]
  · funext i; by_cases h : i = j
    · subst h; simp [swapJA]
    · simp [swapJA, Function.update]

lemma sum_univ_eq_sum_univ_of_involutive
    {α : Type _} [Fintype α] {δ : Type _} [AddCommMonoid δ]
    (e : α → α) (he : Function.Involutive e) (f : α → δ) :
    (∑ x : α, f (e x)) = ∑ x : α, f x := by
  simpa using
    (Finset.sum_bij (s := Finset.univ) (t := Finset.univ)
      (fun x _ => e x) (by intro x _; simp))
      (by intro x₁ _ x₂ _ hxe; have := congrArg e hxe; simpa [he x₁, he x₂] using this)
      (by intro y _; exact ⟨e y, Finset.mem_univ _, he y⟩)
      (by intro _ _; rfl)

end Aux

-- ============================================================================
-- Product PMF & Coordinate Independence
-- ============================================================================

section Core

variable {ι : Type uι} [DecidableEq ι]
variable {A : ι → Type uA}

-- ---- Product PMF --------------------------------------------------------

open Classical in
/-- Product PMF over a finite index type: independently sample each coordinate. -/

file.content:
import Math.Probability
import Mathlib.Probability.ProbabilityMassFunction.Constructions

set_option autoImplicit false

/-!
# PMF Utilities

Canonical home for generic PMF utilities: pushforward, bind congruence, support
extensionality, conditioning (`condOn`), and sequential fold/bind composition.
Does not cover product structure (see `PMFProduct.lean`).
-/

namespace Math
namespace ProbabilityMassFunction

variable {α β γ : Type*}

noncomputable def pushforward (μ : PMF α) (f : α → β) : PMF β :=
  μ.bind (fun a => PMF.pure (f a))

theorem pushforward_comp
    (μ : PMF α) (f : α → β) (g : β → γ) :
    pushforward (pushforward μ f) g = pushforward μ (g ∘ f) := by
  simp [pushforward, PMF.bind_bind, Function.comp]

theorem pushforward_pushforward
    (μ : PMF α) (f : α → β) (g : β → γ) :
    pushforward (pushforward μ f) g = pushforward μ (g ∘ f) :=
  pushforward_comp μ f g

theorem pushforward_id (μ : PMF α) :
    pushforward μ id = μ := by
  simp [pushforward]

theorem pushforward_pure (a : α) (f : α → β) :
    pushforward (PMF.pure a) f = PMF.pure (f a) := by
  simp [pushforward]

theorem bind_pure_eq_pushforward
    (μ : PMF α) (f : α → β) :
    μ.bind (fun a => PMF.pure (f a)) = pushforward μ f := by
  rfl

theorem bind_assoc
    (μ : PMF α) (f : α → PMF β) (g : β → PMF γ) :
    (μ.bind f).bind g = μ.bind (fun a => (f a).bind g) := by
  exact PMF.bind_bind (p := μ) (f := f) (g := g)

theorem pushforward_bind
    (μ : PMF α) (k : α → PMF β) (f : β → γ) :
    pushforward (μ.bind k) f = μ.bind (fun a => pushforward (k a) f) := by
  simp [pushforward, PMF.bind_bind]

theorem bind_congr_on_support
    (μ : PMF α) (f g : α → PMF β)
    (hfg : ∀ a, a ∈ μ.support → f a = g a) :
    μ.bind f = μ.bind g := by
  ext b
  simp only [PMF.bind_apply]
  refine tsum_congr (fun a => ?_)
  by_cases ha0 : μ a = 0
  · simp [ha0]
  · have haS : a ∈ μ.support := by
      simpa [PMF.mem_support_iff] using ha0
    rw [hfg a haS]

theorem bind_congr_of_ne_zero
    (μ : PMF α) (f g : α → PMF β)
    (hfg : ∀ a, μ a ≠ 0 → f a = g a) :
    μ.bind f = μ.bind g := by
  exact bind_congr_on_support μ f g (fun a ha => hfg a (by simpa [PMF.mem_support_iff] using ha))

theorem expect_congr_on_support
    {Ω : Type*} (μ : PMF Ω) (f g : Ω → ℝ)
    (hfg : ∀ a, a ∈ μ.support → f a = g a) :
    Math.Probability.expect μ f = Math.Probability.expect μ g := by
  unfold Math.Probability.expect
  refine tsum_congr (fun a => ?_)
  by_cases ha0 : μ a = 0

import Mathlib.Probability.ProbabilityMassFunction.Constructions
import Math.ProbabilityMassFunction

/-!
# Iterated PMF kernel

A probabilistic analogue of `Function.iterate` for PMF kernels:
`Math.PMFIter.iter step n b` is the distribution of `step` applied `n`
times starting from `b`.

The combinator factors out a pattern that appears inlined across several
kernel-iteration definitions in this library (see e.g.
`Math.ParameterizedChain.pureRun`, `StepwiseGame.runDist`,
`Languages.MAID.SOS.iterDist`).
-/

set_option autoImplicit false

namespace Math
namespace PMFIter

open Math.ProbabilityMassFunction

variable {B : Type*}

/-- Iterated PMF kernel: `iter step n b` is the distribution of `step`
applied `n` times starting from `b`. -/
noncomputable def iter (step : B → PMF B) : Nat → B → PMF B
  | 0,     b => PMF.pure b
  | n + 1, b => (step b).bind (iter step n)

@[simp] theorem iter_zero (step : B → PMF B) (b : B) :
    iter step 0 b = PMF.pure b := rfl

theorem iter_succ (step : B → PMF B) (b : B) (n : Nat) :
    iter step (n + 1) b = (step b).bind (iter step n) := rfl

theorem iter_succ' (step : B → PMF B) (b : B) (n : Nat) :
    iter step (n + 1) b = (iter step n b).bind step := by
  induction n generalizing b with
  | zero =>
    show (step b).bind (iter step 0) = (PMF.pure b).bind step
    simp [PMF.pure_bind, PMF.bind_pure]
  | succ n ih =>
    show (step b).bind (iter step (n + 1))
        = ((step b).bind (iter step n)).bind step
    rw [PMF.bind_bind]
    congr 1
    funext b'
    exact (ih b').symm

/-- If `step b` is a fixed point of the kernel, iteration from `b` stays
at `b`. -/
theorem iter_of_terminal {step : B → PMF B} {b : B}
    (h : step b = PMF.pure b) (n : Nat) :
    iter step n b = PMF.pure b := by
  induction n with
  | zero => rfl
  | succ n ih =>
    rw [iter_succ, h, PMF.pure_bind]
    exact ih

/-- Extra fuel beyond the closure budget is harmless when every state in
the support of `iter step n b` is a fixed point of `step`. -/
theorem iter_stable_after_terminal
    {step : B → PMF B} {b : B} {n m : Nat}
    (h : ∀ b' ∈ (iter step n b).support, step b' = PMF.pure b')
    (hle : n ≤ m) :
    iter step m b = iter step n b := by
  induction m, hle using Nat.le_induction with
  | base => rfl
  | succ k _ ih =>
    rw [iter_succ', ih,
        bind_congr_on_support (iter step n b) step PMF.pure h]
    exact PMF.bind_pure _

end PMFIter
end Math

at `b`. -/

    (h : step b = PMF.pure b) (n : Nat) :
    iter step n b = PMF.pure b := by
  induction n with
  | zero => rfl
  | succ n ih =>
    rw [iter_succ, h, PMF.pure_bind]
    exact ih

/-- Extra fuel beyond the closure budget is harmless when every state in
the support of `iter step n b` is a fixed point of `step`. -/
theorem iter_stable_after_terminal
    {step : B → PMF B} {b : B} {n m : Nat}
    (h : ∀ b' ∈ (iter step n b).support, step b' = PMF.pure b')
    (hle : n ≤ m) :
    iter step m b = iter step n b := by
  induction m, hle using Nat.le_induction with
  | base => rfl
  | succ k _ ih =>
    rw [iter_succ', ih,
        bind_congr_on_support (iter step n b) step PMF.pure h]
    exact PMF.bind_pure _

end PMFIter
end Math

import Mathlib.Probability.ProbabilityMassFunction.Constructions
import Math.ProbabilityMassFunction

/-!
# Iterated PMF kernel

A probabilistic analogue of `Function.iterate` for PMF kernels:
`Math.PMFIter.iter step n b` is the distribution of `step` applied `n`
times starting from `b`.

The combinator factors out a pattern that appears inlined across several
kernel-iteration definitions in this library (see e.g.
`Math.ParameterizedChain.pureRun`, `StepwiseGame.runDist`,
`Languages.MAID.SOS.iterDist`).
-/

set_option autoImplicit false

namespace Math
namespace PMFIter

open Math.ProbabilityMassFunction

variable {B : Type*}

/-- Iterated PMF kernel: `iter step n b` is the distribution of `step`
applied `n` times starting from `b`. -/
noncomputable def iter (step : B → PMF B) : Nat → B → PMF B
  | 0,     b => PMF.pure b
  | n + 1, b => (step b).bind (iter step n)

@[simp] theorem iter_zero (step : B → PMF B) (b : B) :
    iter step 0 b = PMF.pure b := rfl

theorem iter_succ (step : B → PMF B) (b : B) (n : Nat) :
    iter step (n + 1) b = (step b).bind (iter step n) := rfl

theorem iter_succ' (step : B → PMF B) (b : B) (n : Nat) :
    iter step (n + 1) b = (iter step n b).bind step := by
  induction n generalizing b with
  | zero =>
    show (step b).bind (iter step 0) = (PMF.pure b).bind step
    simp [PMF.pure_bind, PMF.bind_pure]
  | succ n ih =>
    show (step b).bind (iter step (n + 1))
        = ((step b).bind (iter step n)).bind step
    rw [PMF.bind_bind]
    congr 1
    funext b'
    exact (ih b').symm

/-- If `step b` is a fixed point of the kernel, iteration from `b` stays
at `b`. -/
theorem iter_of_terminal {step : B → PMF B} {b : B}
    (h : step b = PMF.pure b) (n : Nat) :
    iter step n b = PMF.pure b := by
  induction n with
  | zero => rfl
  | succ n ih =>
    rw [iter_succ, h, PMF.pure_bind]
    exact ih

/-- Extra fuel beyond the closure budget is harmless when every state in
the support of `iter step n b` is a fixed point of `step`. -/
theorem iter_stable_after_terminal
    {step : B → PMF B} {b : B} {n m : Nat}
    (h : ∀ b' ∈ (iter step n b).support, step b' = PMF.pure b')
    (hle : n ≤ m) :
    iter step m b = iter step n b := by
  induction m, hle using Nat.le_induction with
  | base => rfl
  | succ k _ ih =>
    rw [iter_succ', ih,
        bind_congr_on_support (iter step n b) step PMF.pure h]
    exact PMF.bind_pure _

end PMFIter
end Math

180	    (extendFrontier S cfg)

182	/-- Extract total assignment from a frontier config (default 0 for unassigned). -/
183	def extractTAssign (S : Struct Player n)
184	    (cfg : FrontierCfg S) : TAssign S :=
185	  fun nd => if h : nd ∈ cfg.assigned then cfg.values ⟨nd, h⟩
186	            else default
187	
188	/-- Frontier evaluation: iterate frontier steps, extract terminal assignment.
189	`n` steps suffice (each step assigns ≥1 node while unassigned nodes remain). -/
190	noncomputable def frontierEval (S : Struct Player n) (sem : Sem S)
191	    (pol : Policy S) : PMF (TAssign S) :=
192	  (Nat.iterate (fun d => d.bind (frontierStepPol S sem pol)) n
193	    (PMF.pure (initialCfg S))).map (extractTAssign S)
194

196	-- Frontier infrastructure lemmas
197	-- ============================================================================

199	/-- An enabled node is not yet assigned. -/
200	theorem frontier_not_assigned (S : Struct Player n) (cfg : FrontierCfg S) (nd : Fin n)
201	    (h : nd ∈ frontier S cfg) : nd ∉ cfg.assigned := by
202	  classical
203	  have : enabled S cfg nd := (Finset.mem_filter.mp h).2
204	  exact this.1

209	    frontier S cfg ⊆ (extendFrontier S cfg vals).assigned := by

file.content:
(extendFrontier S cfg)

/-- Extract total assignment from a frontier config (default 0 for unassigned). -/
def extractTAssign (S : Struct Player n)
    (cfg : FrontierCfg S) : TAssign S :=
  fun nd => if h : nd ∈ cfg.assigned then cfg.values ⟨nd, h⟩
            else default

/-- Frontier evaluation: iterate frontier steps, extract terminal assignment.
`n` steps suffice (each step assigns ≥1 node while unassigned nodes remain). -/
noncomputable def frontierEval (S : Struct Player n) (sem : Sem S)
    (pol : Policy S) : PMF (TAssign S) :=
  (Nat.iterate (fun d => d.bind (frontierStepPol S sem pol)) n
    (PMF.pure (initialCfg S))).map (extractTAssign S)

-- ============================================================================
-- Frontier infrastructure lemmas
-- ============================================================================

/-- An enabled node is not yet assigned. -/
theorem frontier_not_assigned (S : Struct Player n) (cfg : FrontierCfg S) (nd : Fin n)
    (h : nd ∈ frontier S cfg) : nd ∉ cfg.assigned := by
  classical
  have : enabled S cfg nd := (Finset.mem_filter.mp h).2
  exact this.1

/-- After `extendFrontier`, the old frontier is assigned. -/
theorem frontier_sub_extendFrontier_assigned (S : Struct Player n)
    (cfg : FrontierCfg S) (vals : FrontierValues S cfg) :
    frontier S cfg ⊆ (extendFrontier S cfg vals).assigned := by
  intro nd hnd
  simp only [extendFrontier, Finset.mem_union]
  exact Or.inr hnd

/-- After `extendFrontier`, old assigned nodes stay assigned. -/
theorem assigned_sub_extendFrontier (S : Struct Player n)
    (cfg : FrontierCfg S) (vals : FrontierValues S cfg) :
    cfg.assigned ⊆ (extendFrontier S cfg vals).assigned := by
  intro nd hnd
  simp only [extendFrontier, Finset.mem_union]

-- ============================================================================
-- The iteration distribution
-- ============================================================================

/-- The PMF on `FrontierCfg S` after `k` frontier iteration steps. -/
noncomputable def iterDist (S : Struct Player n) (sem : Sem S)
    (pol : Policy S) (k : Nat) : PMF (FrontierCfg S) :=
  Nat.iterate (fun d => d.bind (frontierStepPol S sem pol)) k
    (PMF.pure (initialCfg S))

/-- `initialCfg S` equals `cfgOfAssign S a ∅` for any `a`. -/
theorem initialCfg_eq_cfgOfAssign (S : Struct Player n) (a : TAssign S) :
    initialCfg S = cfgOfAssign S a ∅ := by
  simp only [initialCfg, cfgOfAssign]
  congr 1
  funext ⟨nd, hnd⟩
  simp at hnd

/-- `layerAssigned` at step 0 is empty. -/
theorem layerAssigned_zero (S : Struct Player n) :
    layerAssigned S 0 = ∅ := rfl

/-- `layerAssigned` at step k+1 is layerAssigned k ∪ frontierOfAssigned S (layerAssigned S k). -/
theorem layerAssigned_succ (S : Struct Player n) (k : Nat) :
    layerAssigned S (k + 1) =
      layerAssigned S k ∪ frontierOfAssigned S (layerAssigned S k) := rfl

/-- Any `FrontierCfg` equals `cfgOfAssign` applied to its own `extractTAssign`

439	    have : cfg = initialCfg S := by
440	      by_contra hne; exact h (if_neg hne)
441	    rw [this]; rfl
442	  | succ k ih =>

444	    have hrew : iterDist S sem pol (k + 1) =

-- Support lemma: iterDist is zero for wrong assigned set
-- ============================================================================

open Classical Math.ProbabilityMassFunction Math.PMFProduct in
/-- Configs in the support of `iterDist k` have
`assigned = layerAssigned S k`. -/
theorem iterDist_assigned (S : Struct Player n) (sem : Sem S)
    (pol : Policy S) (k : Nat) (hk : k ≤ n)
    (cfg : FrontierCfg S)
    (h : iterDist S sem pol k cfg ≠ 0) :
    cfg.assigned = layerAssigned S k := by
  induction k generalizing cfg with
  | zero =>
    simp only [iterDist, Nat.iterate, PMF.pure_apply] at h
    have : cfg = initialCfg S := by
      by_contra hne; exact h (if_neg hne)
    rw [this]; rfl
  | succ k ih =>
    -- iterDist (k+1) = (iterDist k).bind (frontierStepPol ...)
    have hrew : iterDist S sem pol (k + 1) =
        (iterDist S sem pol k).bind
          (frontierStepPol S sem pol) := by
      simp only [iterDist]
      rw [Function.iterate_succ_apply']
    rw [hrew] at h
    rw [PMF.bind_apply] at h
    -- Some term in the tsum is nonzero
    have hne := mt ENNReal.tsum_eq_zero.mpr h
    push Not at hne
    obtain ⟨cfg', hcfg'⟩ := hne
    have h1 : iterDist S sem pol k cfg' ≠ 0 :=
      left_ne_zero_of_mul hcfg'
    have h2 : frontierStepPol S sem pol cfg' cfg ≠ 0 :=
      right_ne_zero_of_mul hcfg'
    have hassign' := ih (Nat.le_of_succ_le hk) cfg' h1
    -- frontierStepPol cfg' cfg ≠ 0 → ∃ vals, cfg = extend
    rw [frontierStepPol_apply] at h2
    have hne2 := mt ENNReal.tsum_eq_zero.mpr h2
    push Not at hne2
    obtain ⟨vals, hvals⟩ := hne2

1	import GameTheory.Core.KernelGame
2	import GameTheory.Languages.InfoModel.InfoGame
3	import GameTheory.Languages.InfoModel.Simulation
4	import GameTheory.Languages.MultiRound.SOS
5	import Math.PMFProduct
6	import Math.Probability
7	
8	/-!
9	# GameTheory.Languages.MultiRound.Compile
10	
11	Compilation-facing semantic core for sequential/protocol languages.
12	
13	This file provides a small repeated-step semantics:
14	- `PreKernelStep`: one semantic step (state, local views, joint controls)
15	- `StepwiseGame`: initial distribution + finite horizon + terminal utility
16	- `toKernelGame`: collapse to a `KernelGame`
17	
18	Language-native protocol syntax should compile into this layer, and then
19	further into the generic semantic targets when theorem transport is needed.
20	-/
21	
22	namespace GameTheory
23	
24	open Math.Probability
25	
26	open scoped BigOperators
27	open Math.PMFProduct
28	
29	section Core
30	
31	variable {ι : Type} [Fintype ι]
32	
33	/-- One semantic step: each controller observes a view of state, samples an action,
34	and all actions combine into a stochastic next state. -/
35	structure PreKernelStep (ι : Type) [Fintype ι] where
36	  State : Type
37	  View : ι → Type
38	  Act : ι → Type
39	  [fAct : ∀ i, Fintype (Act i)]
40	  observe : State → (i : ι) → View i
41	  combine : State → (∀ i, Act i) → PMF State
42	
43	attribute [instance] PreKernelStep.fAct
44	
45	/-- A policy chooses a distribution over actions from each controller view. -/
46	abbrev Policy (K : PreKernelStep ι) : Type :=
47	  (i : ι) → K.View i → PMF (K.Act i)
48	
49	namespace PreKernelStep
50	
51	variable (K : PreKernelStep ι)
52	
53	open Classical in
54	/-- Joint action law at state `s` induced by policy `π` (independent local sampling). -/
55	noncomputable def jointActDist (π : Policy K) (s : K.State) : PMF (∀ i, K.Act i) :=
56	  pmfPi (fun i => π i (K.observe s i))
57	
58	/-- One-step state-transition kernel induced by policy `π`. -/
59	noncomputable def stepKernel (π : Policy K) : Kernel K.State K.State :=
60	  fun s => (K.jointActDist π s).bind (K.combine s)
61	
62	end PreKernelStep
63	
64	/-- A finite-horizon sequential game built from a repeated semantic step. -/
65	structure StepwiseGame (ι : Type) [Fintype ι] where
66	  Step : PreKernelStep ι
67	  init : PMF Step.State
68	  horizon : Nat
69	  utility : Step.State → Payoff ι
70	
71	namespace StepwiseGame
72	
73	variable (G : StepwiseGame ι)
74	
75	/-- Run distribution after `horizon` repeated steps under policy `π`. -/
76	noncomputable def runDist (π : Policy G.Step) : PMF G.Step.State :=
77	  Nat.rec G.init (fun _ acc => acc.bind (G.Step.stepKernel π)) G.horizon
78	
79	@[simp] theorem runDist_zero (π : Policy G.Step) (h0 : G.horizon = 0) :
80	    G.runDist π = G.init := by
81	  simp [runDist, h0]
82	
83	/-- Collapse a sequential game into its profile-to-outcome kernel semantics. -/
84	noncomputable def toKernelGame : KernelGame ι where
85	  Strategy := fun i => G.Step.View i → PMF (G.Step.Act i)
86	  Outcome := G.Step.State
87	  utility := G.utility
88	  outcomeKernel := fun π => G.runDist π
89	
90	@[simp] theorem toKernelGame_outcomeKernel (π : Policy G.Step) :
91	    G.toKernelGame.outcomeKernel π = G.runDist π := rfl
92	
93	end StepwiseGame
94	
95	section DeterministicPolicy

import GameTheory.Core.KernelGame
import GameTheory.Languages.InfoModel.InfoGame
import GameTheory.Languages.InfoModel.Simulation
import GameTheory.Languages.MultiRound.SOS
import Math.PMFProduct
import Math.Probability

/-!
# GameTheory.Languages.MultiRound.Compile

Compilation-facing semantic core for sequential/protocol languages.

This file provides a small repeated-step semantics:
- `PreKernelStep`: one semantic step (state, local views, joint controls)
- `StepwiseGame`: initial distribution + finite horizon + terminal utility
- `toKernelGame`: collapse to a `KernelGame`

Language-native protocol syntax should compile into this layer, and then
further into the generic semantic targets when theorem transport is needed.
-/

namespace GameTheory

open Math.Probability

open scoped BigOperators
open Math.PMFProduct

section Core

variable {ι : Type} [Fintype ι]

/-- One semantic step: each controller observes a view of state, samples an action,
and all actions combine into a stochastic next state. -/
structure PreKernelStep (ι : Type) [Fintype ι] where
  State : Type
  View : ι → Type
  Act : ι → Type
  [fAct : ∀ i, Fintype (Act i)]
  observe : State → (i : ι) → View i
  combine : State → (∀ i, Act i) → PMF State

attribute [instance] PreKernelStep.fAct

/-- A policy chooses a distribution over actions from each controller view. -/
abbrev Policy (K : PreKernelStep ι) : Type :=
  (i : ι) → K.View i → PMF (K.Act i)

namespace PreKernelStep

variable (K : PreKernelStep ι)

open Classical in
/-- Joint action law at state `s` induced by policy `π` (independent local sampling). -/
noncomputable def jointActDist (π : Policy K) (s : K.State) : PMF (∀ i, K.Act i) :=
  pmfPi (fun i => π i (K.observe s i))

/-- One-step state-transition kernel induced by policy `π`. -/
noncomputable def stepKernel (π : Policy K) : Kernel K.State K.State :=
  fun s => (K.jointActDist π s).bind (K.combine s)

end PreKernelStep

/-- A finite-horizon sequential game built from a repeated semantic step. -/
structure StepwiseGame (ι : Type) [Fintype ι] where
  Step : PreKernelStep ι
  init : PMF Step.State
  horizon : Nat
  utility : Step.State → Payoff ι

namespace StepwiseGame

variable (G : StepwiseGame ι)

/-- Run distribution after `horizon` repeated steps under policy `π`. -/
noncomputable def runDist (π : Policy G.Step) : PMF G.Step.State :=
  Nat.rec G.init (fun _ acc => acc.bind (G.Step.stepKernel π)) G.horizon

@[simp] theorem runDist_zero (π : Policy G.Step) (h0 : G.horizon = 0) :
    G.runDist π = G.init := by
  simp [runDist, h0]

/-- Collapse a sequential game into its profile-to-outcome kernel semantics. -/
noncomputable def toKernelGame : KernelGame ι where
  Strategy := fun i => G.Step.View i → PMF (G.Step.Act i)
  Outcome := G.Step.State
  utility := G.utility
  outcomeKernel := fun π => G.runDist π

@[simp] theorem toKernelGame_outcomeKernel (π : Policy G.Step) :
    G.toKernelGame.outcomeKernel π = G.runDist π := rfl

end StepwiseGame

section DeterministicPolicy

let s := (ss.getLast?).getD I.init
  let v : ∀ i, I.LocalTrace i := fun i => I.projectStates i ss
  (b v).bind fun a =>
    D.nextState a s

/-- Bounded run distribution of length exactly `k`, storing just the state trace. -/
noncomputable def runDist (D : Dynamics I)
    [DecidableEq ι] [Fintype ι] [∀ i, Fintype (Option (Act i))]
    (k : Nat) (b : BehavioralProfile I) : PMF (List σ) :=
  Nat.rec (PMF.pure [I.init])
    (fun _ rec =>
      rec.bind (fun ss =>
        Math.ProbabilityMassFunction.pushforward (D.stepDist b ss)
          (fun t => ss ++ [t])))
    k

/-- Pure-profile run distribution via `pureToBehavioral`. -/
noncomputable def runDistPure (D : Dynamics I)
    [DecidableEq ι] [Fintype ι] [∀ i, Fintype (Option (Act i))]
    (k : Nat) (π : PureProfile I) : PMF (List σ) :=
  D.runDist k (pureToBehavioral I π)

/-- Outcome distribution from bounded behavioral runs. -/
noncomputable def evalBehavioral (D : Dynamics I)
    [DecidableEq ι] [Fintype ι] [∀ i, Fintype (Option (Act i))]
    (k : Nat) (b : BehavioralProfile I) : PMF I.Outcome :=
  Math.ProbabilityMassFunction.pushforward (D.runDist k b) I.outcomeOfStates

/-- Outcome distribution from bounded pure runs. -/
noncomputable def evalPure (D : Dynamics I)
    [DecidableEq ι] [Fintype ι] [∀ i, Fintype (Option (Act i))]
    (k : Nat) (π : PureProfile I) : PMF I.Outcome :=
  Math.ProbabilityMassFunction.pushforward (D.runDistPure k π) I.outcomeOfStates

/-- Independent one-step realizability. -/
def OneStepIndependentRealizable (D : Dynamics I)
    [DecidableEq ι] [Fintype ι] [∀ i, Fintype (Option (Act i))]
    (ν : PMF (PureProfile I)) (n : Nat) : Prop :=
  ∃ b : BehavioralProfile I,
    (ν.bind (fun π =>
      (D.runDistPure n π).bind (fun ss =>
        Math.ProbabilityMassFunction.pushforward
          (D.stepDist b ss)
          (fun t => ss ++ [t])))) =

(b : BehavioralProfile O) (ss : List σ) : PMF σ :=
  let s := O.lastState ss
  (O.jointActionDist b ss).bind fun a => O.step s (O.castJointAction ss a)

/-- Distribution on state traces of length `k + 1` obtained by running the
machine for `k` steps under behavioral profile `b`, starting from `[init]`. -/
noncomputable def runDist (O : ObsModelCore ι σ Obs Act)
    [DecidableEq ι] [Fintype ι] [∀ i o, Fintype (Act i o)]
    (k : Nat) (b : BehavioralProfile O) : PMF (List σ) :=
  Nat.rec (PMF.pure [O.init])
    (fun _ rec =>
      rec.bind (fun ss =>
        pushforward (O.stepDist b ss) (fun t => ss ++ [t])))
    k

/-- Pure-profile run distribution via `pureToBehavioral`. -/
noncomputable def runDistPure (O : ObsModelCore ι σ Obs Act)
    [DecidableEq ι] [Fintype ι] [∀ i o, Fintype (Act i o)]
    (k : Nat) (π : PureProfile O) : PMF (List σ) :=
  O.runDist k (O.pureToBehavioral π)

/-- The pure step function: directly applies `O.step` at the deterministic action. -/
noncomputable def pureStep (O : ObsModelCore ι σ Obs Act)
    [DecidableEq ι] [Fintype ι] [∀ i o, Fintype (Act i o)]
    (π : PureProfile O) (ss : List σ) : PMF σ :=
  O.stepDist (O.pureToBehavioral π) ss

/-- `runDistPure` equals `pureRun` applied to `pureStep`. -/
theorem runDistPure_eq_pureRun (O : ObsModelCore ι σ Obs Act)
    [DecidableEq ι] [Fintype ι] [∀ i o, Fintype (Act i o)]
    (k : Nat) (π : PureProfile O) :
    O.runDistPure k π = pureRun (O.pureStep) O.init k π := rfl

/-- Two behavioral profiles that agree at all info states visited by `runDist m β₁`
(for all `m`) produce the same `runDist k` for every `k`. -/

@[simp] theorem initialSegment_steps (h : G.History) (n : Nat) :
    (h.initialSegment n).steps = h.steps.take n := rfl

theorem initialSegment_isPrefix (h : G.History) (n : Nat) :
    (h.initialSegment n).IsPrefix h := by
  refine ⟨h.steps.drop n, ?_⟩
  simp [initialSegment, List.take_append_drop]

theorem initialSegment_length
    (h : G.History) {n : Nat} (hn : n ≤ h.steps.length) :
    (h.initialSegment n).steps.length = n := by
  simp [initialSegment, List.length_take, hn]

/-- Horizon-bounded run distribution on realized histories induced by a legal
behavioral profile. Terminal histories absorb. -/
noncomputable def runDistFrom
    (G : FOSG ι W Act PrivObs PubObs)
    [Fintype ι] [∀ i, Fintype (Option (Act i))] [Fintype W]
    [DecidablePred G.terminal]
    (σ : G.LegalBehavioralProfile) : Nat → G.History → PMF G.History
  | 0, h => PMF.pure h
  | n + 1, h =>
      if hterm : G.terminal h.lastState then
        PMF.pure h
      else
        (G.legalActionLaw σ h hterm).bind fun a =>
          (G.transition h.lastState a).bind fun dst =>
            runDistFrom G σ n (h.extendByOutcome a dst)

@[simp] theorem runDistFrom_zero
    {G : FOSG ι W Act PrivObs PubObs}
    [Fintype ι] [∀ i, Fintype (Option (Act i))] [Fintype W]
    [DecidablePred G.terminal]
    (σ : G.LegalBehavioralProfile) (h : G.History) :
    runDistFrom G σ 0 h = PMF.pure h := rfl

content.text:
Survey results: there are real candidates but each has heavy downstream proof dependence on its current shape, so migration would be churn without proof-simplification payoff:

| Candidate | File | Shape match | Migration cost |
|---|---|---|---|
| `StepwiseGame.runDist` | `MultiRound/Compile.lean:76` | Clean: `G.init.bind (iter step horizon)` | Heavy — used in `MultiRound/Kuhn.lean`, `MAID/Kuhn.lean`, `Bridges/FOSG/{SerialExec,AugmentedEFG}.lean`, `CompileObsLinAdequacy.lean` with custom unfold idioms |
| `History.runDistFrom` | `FOSG/Compile.lean:118` | Matches `iter` with terminal absorption — `iter_of_terminal` would discharge the `if terminal` branch | Custom recursion with `if` baked in; rewriting the def changes all `runDistFrom_succ_*` simp lemmas |
| `iterDist`, `frontierEval` | `MAID/{FrontierEval,SOS}.lean` | Direct: `Nat.iterate (·.bind step) n (PMF.pure b)` ≡ `iter step n b` | 30+ proofs in `FrontierEval.lean` use `simp [iterDist, Nat.iterate, ...]` and `Function.iterate_succ_apply'` idioms |
| `Dynamics.runDist`, `ObsModelCore.runDist`, `ParameterizedChain.pureRun`, `InfoModel.SemanticForm.runDist` | various | **Don't fit** — they track state-trace `List σ` with append-singleton bind, not Kleisli-on-`B` |

My recommendation: leave existing defs as-is; `Math.PMFIter.iter` is available for *new* kernel-iteration code, and a migration becomes worthwhile only when someone is already touching nearby proofs (e.g., next time `StepwiseGame.runDist`'s API needs an `iter_of_terminal`-shaped lemma, swap the def then). Worth one bridge lemma if you want, e.g. `runDist_eq_iter : G.runDist π = G.init.bind (iter (G.Step.stepKernel π) G.horizon)`, which lets clients pull `iter` lemmas in without changing the def.

Want me to add that one bridge lemma for `StepwiseGame.runDist`, or stop here?

file.content:
import GameTheory.Core.GameIsomorphism
import Math.Probability
import GameTheory.Concepts.SolutionConcepts

/-!
# Game Morphisms

Morphisms between kernel-based games: structure-preserving maps that relate the
strategic structure of different games.

This file is distribution-first:
- morphisms preserve utility distributions (`udist`) under profile mapping;
- EU-based corollaries (Nash, dominance) are stated in explicitly EU-specialized
  structures.

## Main definitions

* `KernelGame.Morphism` -- strategy map preserving `udist`
* `KernelGame.EUMorphism` -- morphism with additional EU preservation
* `KernelGame.EUGameIsomorphism` -- game isomorphism with additional EU preservation

## Main EU corollaries

* `KernelGame.EUMorphism.nash_of_nash`
* `KernelGame.EUGameIsomorphism.nash_iff`
* `KernelGame.EUGameIsomorphism.dominant_iff`
-/

namespace GameTheory

open Math.Probability

namespace KernelGame

variable {ι : Type}

-- ============================================================================
-- Game morphisms (distribution-preserving)
-- ============================================================================

/-- A morphism from game `G` to game `H`: per-player strategy maps that preserve
    the induced joint utility distribution. -/
structure Morphism (G H : KernelGame ι) where
  /-- Per-player strategy map. -/
  stratMap : ∀ i, G.Strategy i → H.Strategy i
  /-- Utility-distribution preservation under mapped profiles. -/
  udist_preserved : ∀ (σ : Profile G),
    H.udist (fun i => stratMap i (σ i)) = G.udist σ

namespace Morphism

/-- Identity morphism. -/
def id (G : KernelGame ι) : Morphism G G where
  stratMap := fun _i => _root_.id
  udist_preserved := by intro σ; rfl

/-- Composition of morphisms. -/
def comp {G H K : KernelGame ι} (g : Morphism H K) (f : Morphism G H) : Morphism G K where
  stratMap := fun i => g.stratMap i ∘ f.stratMap i
  udist_preserved := by
    intro σ
    let τ : Profile H := fun i => f.stratMap i (σ i)
    have hg : K.udist (fun i => g.stratMap i (τ i)) = H.udist τ := g.udist_preserved τ
    have hf : H.udist τ = G.udist σ := by simpa [τ] using f.udist_preserved σ
    simpa [τ, Function.comp] using hg.trans hf

@[simp] theorem id_comp {G H : KernelGame ι} (f : Morphism G H) :
    comp (id H) f = f := by
  cases f
  rfl

@[simp] theorem comp_id {G H : KernelGame ι} (f : Morphism G H) :
    comp f (id G) = f := by
  cases f
  rfl

theorem comp_assoc {G H K L : KernelGame ι}
    (h : Morphism K L) (g : Morphism H K) (f : Morphism G H) :
    comp h (comp g f) = comp (comp h g) f := by
  cases h; cases g; cases f
  rfl

end Morphism

/-- `udist` preservation implies per-player utility-distribution preservation. -/
theorem Morphism.udistPlayer_preserved {G H : KernelGame ι} (f : Morphism G H)
    (σ : Profile G) (who : ι) :
    H.udistPlayer (fun i => f.stratMap i (σ i)) who = G.udistPlayer σ who := by
  have h :=
    congrArg (fun d : PMF (Payoff ι) => d.bind (fun u => PMF.pure (u who))) (f.udist_preserved σ)
  simpa [KernelGame.udistPlayer_eq_udist_bind] using h

-- ============================================================================
-- EU-specialized morphisms
-- ============================================================================

/-- A morphism that additionally preserves expected utility values. -/
structure EUMorphism (G H : KernelGame ι) extends Morphism G H where
  /-- Expected-utility preservation under mapped profiles. -/
  eu_preserved : ∀ (σ : Profile G) (who : ι),
    H.eu (fun i => stratMap i (σ i)) who = G.eu σ who

namespace EUMorphism

/-- Identity EU morphism. -/
def id (G : KernelGame ι) : EUMorphism G G where
  toMorphism := Morphism.id G
  eu_preserved := by intro σ who; rfl

/-- Composition of EU morphisms. -/
def comp {G H K : KernelGame ι} (g : EUMorphism H K) (f : EUMorphism G H) : EUMorphism G K where
  toMorphism := Morphism.comp g.toMorphism f.toMorphism
  eu_preserved := by
    intro σ who
    let τ : Profile H := fun i => f.stratMap i (σ i)
    have hg : K.eu (fun i => g.stratMap i (τ i)) who = H.eu τ who := g.eu_preserved τ who
    have hf : H.eu τ who = G.eu σ who := by simpa [τ] using f.eu_preserved σ who
    simpa [τ, Function.comp] using hg.trans hf

@[simp] theorem id_comp {G H : KernelGame ι} (f : EUMorphism G H) :
    comp (id H) f = f := by
  cases f
  rfl

@[simp] theorem comp_id {G H : KernelGame ι} (f : EUMorphism G H) :
    comp f (id G) = f := by
  cases f
  rfl

theorem comp_assoc {G H K L : KernelGame ι}
    (h : EUMorphism K L) (g : EUMorphism H K) (f : EUMorphism G H) :
    comp h (comp g f) = comp (comp h g) f := by
  cases h; cases g; cases f
  rfl

end EUMorphism

open Classical in
/-- EU corollary: if a morphism preserves EU values, Nash pulls back. -/
theorem EUMorphism.nash_of_nash {G H : KernelGame ι} (f : EUMorphism G H)
    {σ : Profile G}
    (hN : H.IsNash (fun i => f.stratMap i (σ i))) :
    G.IsNash σ := by
  intro who s'
  have h := hN who (f.stratMap who s')
  have h1 := f.eu_preserved σ who
  have h2 : H.eu (Function.update (fun i => f.stratMap i (σ i)) who (f.stratMap who s')) who =
      G.eu (Function.update σ who s') who := by
    rw [← f.eu_preserved (Function.update σ who s') who]
    congr 2
    funext j
    simpa using
      (Function.apply_update
        (f := f.stratMap) (g := σ) (i := who) (v := s') (j := j)).symm
  linarith

-- ============================================================================
-- EU-specialized game isomorphisms
-- ============================================================================
